event: Polynesian voyagers, descendants of Austronesian seafarers from Southeast Asia, begin settling the islands during the great eastward expansion across the Pacific.
Polynesian settlers from Tahiti, the Society Islands and the Marquesas establish permanent communities across the southern Cook Islands, especially Rarotonga.
Rarotonga becomes a major staging point for long-distance ocean voyaging, with oral tradition recording the canoes that later journeyed to settle Aotearoa (New Zealand).
Distinct island chiefdoms and an elaborate system of ariki (paramount chiefs) develop, organising land, marae worship and inter-island relations.
Maori tradition records canoes such as Tainui and Te Arawa departing from Rarotonga's Avana harbour for the settlement of New Zealand.
Spanish explorer Alvaro de Mendana sights Pukapuka in the northern group, the first recorded European contact with the islands.
Portuguese navigator Pedro Fernandes de Queiros, sailing for Spain, sights Rakahanga in the northern Cook Islands.
British captain James Cook sights the southern islands during his Pacific voyages, beginning the European charting of the group later named after him.
Mutineers from HMS Bounty visit Aitutaki, and the islands become known to the wider European world through such encounters.
European traders confirm a sighting of Rarotonga, the largest island, which had eluded earlier explorers.
London Missionary Society teachers from the Society Islands arrive on Aitutaki, beginning the rapid Christianisation of the islands.
Missionary John Williams reaches Rarotonga and helps establish Christianity, transforming island society, law and culture.
Missionaries introduce a code of laws and a centralised mission station, reshaping traditional chiefly authority.
Fearing French expansion from Tahiti, Britain declares the Cook Islands a protectorate and appoints a British resident.
A federal council and parliament are established under Queen Makea Takau Ariki to coordinate the southern islands.
Rarotonga's chiefs petition for British annexation, and the islands are formally ceded to the British Crown.
Britain transfers administration of the Cook Islands to the newly self-governing colony of New Zealand, incorporating the group into its boundaries.
The Cook Islands Act passed by the New Zealand Parliament establishes the legal framework for governing the islands.
United States forces build an airfield and military base on Aitutaki during the Pacific theatre of World War II, linking the islands to transpacific air routes.
A legislative council is created, giving Cook Islanders a formal advisory voice in their own administration for the first time.
The legislative council is reconstituted with an elected majority, expanding local political participation.
The Cook Islands become self-governing in free association with New Zealand on 4 August, with Albert Henry elected as the first premier.
Rarotonga International Airport opens, dramatically boosting tourism and connections with New Zealand and the wider Pacific.
Albert Henry is unseated after an election fraud scandal involving chartered flights to bring voters from New Zealand, and Tom Davis becomes premier.
The Cook Islands and the United States sign a maritime boundary treaty, with the US relinquishing claims to several northern islands.
A new constitution increases the parliament and renames the head of government premier as prime minister.
Tom Davis loses power amid political turbulence as coalition governments and frequent changes of leadership characterise the era.
The Cook Islands sign the Treaty of Rarotonga, establishing the South Pacific Nuclear Free Zone.
The Cook Islands and France sign a friendship treaty, and the territory expands diplomatic relations as a state in free association.
France resumes nuclear testing at Mururoa, prompting strong protest from the Cook Islands and other Pacific nations.
A severe financial and debt crisis forces drastic cuts to the public service and economic restructuring, spurring large-scale emigration to New Zealand.
Cyclone Martin devastates the northern island of Manihiki, killing around twenty people and destroying the pearl-farming industry.
Black pearl farming recovers to become a major export earner alongside tourism, the mainstays of the island economy.
Five cyclones strike within five weeks, causing widespread damage and underscoring the islands' vulnerability to extreme weather.
The Cook Islands strengthen sovereign diplomacy, having established direct relations with China, the European Union and numerous states.
The Cook Islands host the Pacific Islands Forum leaders' meeting, raising the country's regional and international profile.
The government creates Marae Moana, one of the world's largest marine protected areas, covering the country's entire exclusive economic zone.
The World Bank reclassifies the Cook Islands as a high-income economy, reflecting tourism-driven growth.
The COVID-19 pandemic closes borders and halts tourism, the islands' economic lifeline, causing a severe downturn despite no local cases.
Tourism reopens as the Cook Islands re-establish travel links with New Zealand and Australia after the pandemic.
Rising seas, ocean warming and intensifying cyclones drive national climate adaptation planning for the low-lying coral atolls of the northern group.
The Cook Islands sign a comprehensive strategic partnership with China, prompting diplomatic friction with longtime free-association partner New Zealand.
